papergraph.title: Anion-exchange fluorinated ion conductors for stable high-voltage lithium battery.
papergraph.citation: Qiaodong Li et al. (2026) Anion-exchange fluorinated ion conductors for stable high-voltage lithium battery. doi:10.1038/s41467-026-74773-8
papergraph.abstract: The development of high-energy all-solid-state batteries is critically hindered by the electrochemical instability of solid electrolytes against high-voltage oxide positive electrodes. While fluorination is a promising strategy to enhance electrolyte stability, conventional methods are ineffective, resulting in insufficient fluorine content and a debilitating trade-off with ionic conductivity. Here, we report a solid-state anion-exchange strategy that overcomes these limitations by producing core-shell Li-fluoride/LiCl nanocomposite precursors. These precursors enable the synthesis of heavily fluorinated lithium-halide and lithium-sulfide electrolytes that combine high ionic conductivity with good oxidative stability. This stability originates from the formation of a robust, self-limiting LiF-rich interphase at the positive electrode. Consequently, an all-solid-state battery using a Li-rich oxide positive electrode achieves high performance, retaining over 77.5% capacity after 2000 cycles at a high rate of 3 C (1 C = 275 mA/g) and a 5.0 V cutoff. This anion-exchange approach is broadly applicable to other systems and establishes a versatile platform for designing advanced fluorinated materials for next-generation batteries.
papergraph.conclusion: This anion-exchange approach is broadly applicable to other systems and establishes a versatile platform for designing advanced fluorinated materials for next-generation batteries.
strengths: Overcomes trade-off between fluorination and ionic conductivity
Enables synthesis of heavily fluorinated lithium-halide and lithium-sulfide electrolytes with high ionic conductivity and good oxidative stability
Forms a robust, self-limiting LiF-rich interphase at the positive electrode
Achieves high cycling stability: >77.5% capacity retention after 2000 cycles at 3 C and 5.0 V cutoff
improvements: The development of high-energy all-solid-state batteries is critically hindered by the electrochemical instability of solid electrolytes against high-voltage oxide positive electrodes. While fluorination is a promising strategy to enhance electrolyte stability, conventional methods are ineffective,...[truncated]

papergraph.scenario: All-solid-state battery with Li-rich oxide positive electrode
papergraph.benchmark: 77.5% capacity retention after 2000 cycles at 3 C (1 C = 275 mA/g) and 5.0 V cutoff
papergraph.contribution: A solid-state anion-exchange strategy that enables synthesis of heavily fluorinated lithium-based electrolytes combining high ionic conductivity with oxidative stability via LiF-rich interphase formation
papergraph.limitation: The development of high-energy all-solid-state batteries is critically hindered by the electrochemical instability of solid electrolytes against high-voltage oxide positive electrodes. While fluorination is a promising strategy to enhance electrolyte stability, conventional methods are ineffective,...[truncated]

papergraph.title: Anion‐Engineering Toward High‐Voltage‐Stable Halide Superionic Conductors for All‐Solid‐State Lithium Batteries
papergraph.citation: Liang Shen et al. (2024) Anion‐Engineering Toward High‐Voltage‐Stable Halide Superionic Conductors for All‐Solid‐State Lithium Batteries doi:10.1002/adfm.202408571
papergraph.abstract: Halide solid electrolytes (SEs) are attracting strong attention as one of the compelling candidates for the next‐generation of inorganic SEs due to their high ionic conductivity. Nevertheless, unsatisfactory high‐voltage stability restricts the further applications of halide SEs. Herein, the anion‐engineering of F−/O2− is evolved to construct the high‐voltage stable zirconium‐based halide superionic conductors (Li2.5ZrCl5F0.5O0.5, LZCFO). Benefiting from the thermodynamic/kinetic high‐voltage stability of F‐containing SE and the disordered localized structure introduced by O2−, LZCFO displays a practical electrochemical limit of 4.87 V versus Li/Li+ and an ionic conductivity of 1.17 mS cm−1 at 30 °C. With LZCFO and NCM955, the all‐solid‐state lithium battery exhibits a high discharge capacity of 207.1 mAh g−1 at 0.1C and a capacity retention of 81.2% after 500 cycles at 0.5C. The interfacial characterization further demonstrates the formation of the F‐rich cathode–electrolyte interphase (CEI), which inhibits side reactions between the cathode and the SE and boosts excellent cycling stability. This work affords fresh insights on the engineering of SEs with high‐voltage stability, high ionic conductivity, and stable CEI in all‐solid‐state lithium batteries.
papergraph.conclusion: This work affords fresh insights on the engineering of SEs with high‐voltage stability, high ionic conductivity, and stable CEI in all‐solid‐state lithium batteries.
strengths: Practical electrochemical limit of 4.87 V versus Li/Li+
Ionic conductivity of 1.17 mS cm−1 at 30 °C
High discharge capacity of 207.1 mAh g−1 at 0.1C
Capacity retention of 81.2% after 500 cycles at 0.5C
Formation of F-rich cathode–electrolyte interphase (CEI) that inhibits side reactions
papergraph.method: Anion-engineering of F−/O2− to construct zirconium-based halide superionic conductors (Li2.5ZrCl5F0.5O0.5, LZCFO)
papergraph.scenario: All-solid-state lithium battery using LZCFO and NCM955
papergraph.contribution: Engineering of SEs with high-voltage stability, high ionic conductivity, and stable CEI in all-solid-state lithium batteries

keynotes.title: Anion-exchange fluorinated ion conductors for stable high-voltage lithium battery.

keynote.title: Anion-exchange fluorinated ion conductors for stable high-voltage lithium battery
keynote.core_problem: Electrochemical instability of solid electrolytes against high-voltage oxide positive electrodes in all-solid-state batteries; conventional fluorination methods yield insufficient fluorine content and degrade ionic conductivity.
contributions: A solid-state anion-exchange strategy to synthesize heavily fluorinated lithium-halide and lithium-sulfide electrolytes
Core-shell Li-fluoride/LiCl nanocomposite precursors enabling simultaneous high ionic conductivity and oxidative stability
Formation of a robust, self-limiting LiF-rich interphase at the positive electrode
Demonstration of a high-performance all-solid-state battery with >77.5% capacity retention after 2000 cycles at 3 C and 5.0 V cutoff
experiments_or_evidence: All-solid-state battery cycling: >77.5% capacity retention after 2000 cycles at 3 C (1 C = 275 mA/g) with 5.0 V cutoff
Use of Li-rich oxide positive electrode
datasets_or_materials: Heavily fluorinated lithium-halide and lithium-sulfide electrolytes

important_claims.claim: The anion-exchange strategy enables heavily fluorinated electrolytes that combine high ionic conductivity with good oxidative stability.
important_claims.evidence: Synthesis of heavily fluorinated lithium-halide and lithium-sulfide electrolytes via core-shell Li-fluoride/LiCl nanocomposite precursors; demonstrated high-performance cycling with Li-rich oxide cathode.

important_claims.claim: Stability originates from formation of a robust, self-limiting LiF-rich interphase at the positive electrode.
important_claims.evidence: Attributed as the origin of oxidative stability in the text; linked directly to improved battery cycling performance.

important_claims.claim: The anion-exchange approach is broadly applicable to other systems.
important_claims.evidence: Stated explicitly: 'This anion-exchange approach is broadly applicable to other systems and establishes a versatile platform...'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable synthetic route (solid-state anion exchange) for designing fluorinated ion conductors—potentially extendable to other halide/sulfide-based electrolyte families and high-voltage electrode pairings.

keynotes.title: Anion‐Engineering Toward High‐Voltage‐Stable Halide Superionic Conductors for All‐Solid‐State Lithium Batteries

keynote.title: Anion‐Engineering Toward High‐Voltage‐Stable Halide Superionic Conductors for All‐Solid‐State Lithium Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Unsatisfactory high-voltage stability of halide solid electrolytes restricts their application in all-solid-state lithium batteries.
contributions: Development of anion-engineered zirconium-based halide superionic conductor Li2.5ZrCl5F0.5O0.5 (LZCFO)
Achievement of practical electrochemical stability up to 4.87 V vs. Li/Li+
Demonstration of high ionic conductivity (1.17 mS cm−1 at 30 °C)
Formation of a F-rich cathode–electrolyte interphase (CEI) that suppresses side reactions
Realization of stable cycling (81.2% capacity retention after 500 cycles at 0.5C) in LZCFO/NCM955 all-solid-state battery
methods: Anion engineering via co-substitution of F− and O2− into zirconium-based halide lattice
Material synthesis (exact method not specified in abstract)
Electrochemical characterization (cycling, rate capability, electrochemical stability window)
experiments_or_evidence: Electrochemical stability limit measured as 4.87 V vs. Li/Li+
Ionic conductivity measured as 1.17 mS cm−1 at 30 °C
All-solid-state battery performance: 207.1 mAh g−1 discharge capacity at 0.1C; 81.2% capacity retention after 500 cycles at 0.5C
Interfacial characterization confirming F-rich CEI formation

important_claims.claim: LZCFO exhibits a practical electrochemical stability limit of 4.87 V versus Li/Li+
LZCFO achieves ionic conductivity of 1.17 mS cm−1 at 30 °C
LZCFO/NCM955 all-solid-state battery delivers 207.1 mAh g−1 at 0.1C and retains 81.2% capacity after 500 cycles at 0.5C
A F-rich cathode–electrolyte interphase (CEI) forms and inhibits side reactions
important_claims.evidence: Interfacial characterization demonstrating F-rich CEI formation

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a design strategy—dual anion (F−/O2−) engineering—for simultaneously enhancing voltage stability, ionic conductivity, and interfacial compatibility in halide solid electrolytes.
